bytes.topic: Compute Models — VMs vs containers vs serverless vs edge, choosing the right one
bytes.title: AWS Compute Models — Picking Between EC2, ECS, Lambda, and CloudFront Functions
bytes.body: EC2 gives you full control — choose it when you need persistent processes, custom runtimes, or GPU workloads (e.g., g4dn for ML inference). Use Auto Scaling Groups with launch templates for elasticity. ECS on Fargate removes instance management while keeping container semantics — ideal for long-running microservices, background workers, or anything needing >15 min execution. Lambda is the right call for event-driven workloads: S3 triggers, API Gateway backends, SQS consumers. Cold starts bite you on Java/C# — use Provisioned Concurrency or snap-start (Java 21 on Graviton). Lambda's 10 GB memory ceiling and 15-min timeout are hard walls. CloudFront Functions run at 225+ PoPs in sub-millisecond for request manipulation (URL rewrites, A/B headers) — but no network I/O. Lambda@Edge adds full Node.js/Python runtime at the edge with VPC access, at higher latency cost. The decision tree: stateful/GPU → EC2; containerized long-running → Fargate; event-driven <15min → Lambda; pure request transform → CloudFront Functions; edge logic with I/O → Lambda@Edge.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Deploy a Lambda function with Provisioned Concurrency to eliminate cold starts
aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name order-processor \
  --runtime python3.12 \
  --role arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/lambda-exec \
  --handler app.handler \
  --zip-file fileb://function.zip

aws lambda publish-version \
  --function-name order-processor

aws lambda put-provisioned-concurrency-config \
  --function-name order-processor \
  --qualifier 1 \
  --provisioned-concurrent-executions 10

bytes.topic: Networking — VPCs, subnets, security groups, load balancers, DNS
bytes.title: AWS VPC — Locking Down Traffic Between ALB and Private Subnets
bytes.body: A production-grade AWS VPC splits resources across public and private subnets in at least two AZs. Your ALB lives in public subnets (internet-facing), EC2 or ECS tasks in private subnets — never expose compute directly. Security groups are stateful and instance-scoped; NACLs are stateless and subnet-scoped. The right pattern: ALB security group allows inbound 443 from 0.0.0.0/0, and your app security group allows inbound on your app port *only from the ALB security group ID* — not a CIDR. This locks lateral movement even inside the VPC. Private subnets route outbound via a NAT Gateway in the public subnet; never attach an IGW route to a private subnet. For DNS, use Route 53 Alias records (not CNAME) to point your apex domain to the ALB — Alias records are free, resolve at AWS edge, and support the zone apex. Enable VPC DNS resolution (`enableDnsSupport`, `enableDnsHostnames`) or private hosted zones won't resolve. For inter-service traffic, use VPC endpoints (Interface or Gateway) to avoid NAT costs on calls to S3, DynamoDB, or SSM.

bytes.topic: Storage — object vs block vs file storage, when to use each
bytes.title: AWS Storage Decoded — S3 vs EBS vs EFS and When to Use Each
bytes.body:
AWS offers three distinct storage primitives, each optimized for different access patterns.

**S3 (Object Storage):** Infinitely scalable, HTTP-accessible blobs. Use for static assets, backups, data lake raw zones, and ML training datasets. No hierarchy — everything is a key in a bucket. Lifecycle rules, versioning, and S3 Intelligent-Tiering handle cost automatically. Not mountable as a filesystem.

**EBS (Block Storage):** Network-attached SSD/HDD volumes bound to a single EC2 instance (except Multi-Attach on io2). Use for OS volumes, databases (RDS under the hood uses gp3/io2), and any workload needing low-latency random I/O. gp3 is the default — provision IOPS and throughput independently without paying for extra storage.

**EFS (File Storage):** NFS v4.1 filesystem mountable across hundreds of EC2 instances and Lambda concurrently. Use for shared config, CMS media, and containerized workloads on ECS/EKS needing a common volume. Costs ~3× EBS per GB — use EFS Infrequent Access tier to cut that.

Rule of thumb: static/unstructured → S3, single-instance DB/OS → EBS, multi-instance shared files → EFS.
bytes.code_snippet:
#!/bin/bash
# Mount EFS on EC2 (Amazon Linux 2)
EFS_ID="fs-0abc1234"
MOUNT_POINT="/mnt/efs"

sudo yum install -y amazon-efs-utils
sudo mkdir -p $MOUNT_POINT
sudo mount -t efs -o tls $EFS_ID:/ $MOUNT_POINT

# S3 — copy local dir to bucket with storage class override
aws s3 sync ./data s3://my-bucket/data/ \
  --storage-class INTELLIGENT_TIERING

# EBS — create and attach gp3 volume with custom IOPS
aws ec2 create-volume \
  --volume-type gp3 \
  --size 100 \
  --iops 4000 \
  --throughput 250 \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a

bytes.topic: Databases as a Service — managed DB offerings, read replicas, automated backups
bytes.title: AWS RDS — Read Replicas, Automated Backups, and Multi-AZ Without the Ops Burden
bytes.body:
RDS eliminates manual DB operations while giving you production-grade durability. For read scaling, create a read replica with a single API call — RDS uses async binlog replication for MySQL/MariaDB and async streaming replication for PostgreSQL. Promote a replica to standalone in a DR scenario via `promote-read-replica`. Multi-AZ deployments use synchronous replication to a standby in a separate AZ; failover is automatic and typically completes in 60–120 seconds with a DNS flip — your connection string stays the same.

Automated backups use incremental snapshots to S3, retained 1–35 days. Point-in-time recovery (PITR) restores to any second within the retention window by replaying transaction logs against the nearest snapshot. Note: restores always create a new DB instance — you can't restore in-place. For Aurora specifically, `BacktrackWindow` lets you rewind the cluster up to 72 hours without a full restore, which is significantly faster for accidental data mutations.
bytes.code_snippet:
aws rds create-db-instance-read-replica \
  --db-instance-identifier prod-postgres-replica \
  --source-db-instance-identifier prod-postgres \
  --db-instance-class db.r6g.large \
  --availability-zone us-east-1b

# Restore to a point in time (creates new instance)
aws rds restore-db-instance-to-point-in-time \
  --source-db-instance-identifier prod-postgres \
  --target-db-instance-identifier prod-postgres-restored \
  --restore-time 2026-04-17T03:00:00Z

bytes.topic: IAM & Permissions — least privilege, roles vs policies, service accounts, never root
bytes.title: AWS IAM — Least Privilege in Practice: Roles, Policies, and Why Root Should Never Touch Production
bytes.body:
Every AWS principal should do exactly one job. Start with AWS managed policies only to understand the shape of permissions, then replace them with customer-managed policies scoped to specific actions and resources. Never attach policies to IAM users — attach them to roles, then let services, Lambda functions, or EC2 instances assume those roles via `sts:AssumeRole`. This eliminates long-lived credentials entirely.

For service-to-service auth, use IAM roles with resource-based trust policies. An ECS task role with `s3:GetObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/prefix/*` is safer than a wildcard `s3:*` on `*`. Use `aws iam simulate-principal-policy` to validate effective permissions before deploying.

Root credentials must have MFA enabled and hardware keys preferred. Create a break-glass IAM user with AdministratorAccess for emergencies — but never use root for daily ops. Use AWS Organizations SCPs to hard-block `sts:AssumeRole` by root across member accounts. Audit with IAM Access Analyzer to surface unused permissions and external resource exposure automatically.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Simulate effective permissions before deploying
aws iam simulate-principal-policy \
  --policy-source-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/my-lambda-role \
  --action-names s3:PutObject s3:DeleteObject \
  --resource-arns arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/uploads/*

# Find unused permissions in last 90 days
aws iam generate-service-last-accessed-details \
  --arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/my-lambda-role

aws iam get-service-last-accessed-details \
  --job-id <job-id> \
  --query 'ServicesLastAccessed[?TotalAuthenticatedEntities==`0`].ServiceName'

bytes.topic: Cost Optimization — reserved vs spot vs on-demand, rightsizing, budget alerts
bytes.title: AWS Cost Optimization — Reserved vs Spot vs On-Demand, Rightsizing, and Budget Alerts
bytes.body:
AWS pricing has three compute purchase models: On-Demand (pay-per-second, no commitment), Reserved Instances (1- or 3-year commitment, up to 72% savings), and Spot (spare capacity, up to 90% savings but interruptible with a 2-minute warning). Use On-Demand for unpredictable or short-lived workloads. Commit to Reserved Instances (Standard or Convertible) for steady-state services like RDS or always-on EC2 — Convertible RIs let you swap instance families. Run batch jobs, stateless workers, and fault-tolerant services on Spot via EC2 Auto Scaling mixed-instance policies with `SpotAllocationStrategy: capacity-optimized`.

Rightsizing is the highest-ROI lever: AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes CloudWatch utilization and flags over-provisioned instances. Run it via `compute-optimizer get-ec2-instance-recommendations`. For containers, use ECS Fargate right-sizing reports in Cost Explorer.

Set proactive guardrails with AWS Budgets: create cost and usage budgets with SNS-triggered alerts at 80% and 100% thresholds. Use Cost Anomaly Detection (`aws ce create-anomaly-monitor`) to catch unexpected spend spikes before they compound.
bytes.code_snippet:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Create a monthly cost budget with SNS alerts at 80% and 100%
aws budgets create-budget \
  --account-id "$(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text)" \
  --budget '{
    "BudgetName": "monthly-ec2-budget",
    "BudgetLimit": {"Amount": "500", "Unit": "USD"},
    "TimeUnit": "MONTHLY",
    "BudgetType": "COST"
  }' \
  --notifications-with-subscribers '[{
    "Notification": {"NotificationType": "ACTUAL", "ComparisonOperator": "GREATER_THAN", "Threshold": 80},
    "Subscribers": [{"SubscriptionType": "SNS", "Address": "arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:cost-alerts"}]
  }]'

# Get Compute Optimizer rightsizing recommendations
aws compute-optimizer get-ec2-instance-recommendations \
  --filters name=Finding,values=Overprovisioned \
  --query 'instanceRecommendations[*].{Instance:instanceArn,Current:currentInstanceType,Recommended:recommendationOptions[0].instanceType}'

bytes.topic: High Availability — multi-AZ deployments, failover, provider SLAs
bytes.title: AWS High Availability — Multi-AZ Deployments, Failover, and What the SLAs Actually Mean
bytes.body:
AWS Multi-AZ replication separates standby replicas across physically isolated data centers within a region. For RDS Multi-AZ, writes synchronously replicate to the standby before acknowledging — failover triggers automatically within 60–120 seconds via a DNS CNAME flip. This earns a 99.95% SLA vs. 99.9% for Single-AZ deployments.

For EC2, distribute instances across ≥3 AZs in your Auto Scaling Group. Watch out: AZ names (`us-east-1a`) are account-specific mappings — use AZ IDs (`use1-az1`) when coordinating cross-account to ensure you're targeting the same physical zone.

ALB cross-zone load balancing (on by default since 2022) solves AZ skew — without it, an AZ with 2 instances handles the same traffic share as one with 10.

Route 53 failover routing with health checks achieves ~30s cutover when combined with a low TTL. For sub-regional failover, AWS Global Accelerator uses anycast to reroute in under 30s with a 99.99% SLA — bypassing DNS propagation entirely.

RDS failover doesn't change your connection string. Multi-AZ ElastiCache (cluster mode) adds cross-AZ replica promotion with automatic DNS update within seconds.
bytes.code_snippet:
aws rds create-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier prod-postgres \
  --db-instance-class db.r6g.large \
  --engine postgres \
  --engine-version 15.4 \
  --master-username admin \
  --master-user-password "$DB_PASSWORD" \
  --allocated-storage 100 \
  --multi-az \
  --backup-retention-period 7 \
  --deletion-protection \
  --db-subnet-group-name prod-subnet-group

bytes.topic: CDN & Edge — cache setup, invalidation, edge caching strategy
bytes.title: AWS CloudFront — Cache Setup, Invalidation, and Edge Strategy That Actually Works
bytes.body:
CloudFront distributions serve content from 600+ edge locations, but default cache behavior is often misconfigured. Set cache policies using managed policies like `CachingOptimized` (TTL: 86400s) for static assets, and `CachingDisabled` for API origins. Avoid origin-level cache headers overriding your distribution policy — use "Cache policy" over "Use origin cache headers" in behavior settings.

For fine-grained control, create custom cache policies via the Console or CLI, specifying `min-ttl`, `default-ttl`, and `max-ttl` independently. Use `Cache-Control: s-maxage` at the origin to override CloudFront TTL without affecting browser cache.

Invalidations are charged after 1,000 paths/month — use versioned file paths (`main.abc123.js`) instead of frequent `/*` invalidations. When you must invalidate, scope paths tightly: `/static/js/*` beats `/*`.

For dynamic content, configure origin request policies to forward only necessary headers/cookies — forwarding `Cookie: *` disables caching entirely. Use Lambda@Edge or CloudFront Functions for request manipulation at sub-millisecond latency at the edge, keeping heavy logic in Lambda@Edge (viewer/origin request hooks) and lightweight rewrites in CloudFront Functions.
bytes.code_snippet:
aws cloudfront create-invalidation \
  --distribution-id E1EXAMPLE \
  --paths "/static/js/*" "/static/css/*"

# Create a cache policy with custom TTLs
aws cloudfront create-cache-policy \
  --cache-policy-config '{
    "Name": "CustomStaticPolicy",
    "DefaultTTL": 86400,
    "MaxTTL": 31536000,
    "MinTTL": 0,
    "ParametersInCacheKeyAndForwardedToOrigin": {
      "EnableAcceptEncodingGzip": true,
      "EnableAcceptEncodingBrotli": true,
      "HeadersConfig": {"HeaderBehavior": "none"},
      "CookiesConfig": {"CookieBehavior": "none"},
      "QueryStringsConfig": {"QueryStringBehavior": "none"}
    }
  }'

bytes.topic: Serverless — cold starts, timeout limits, stateless design constraints
bytes.title: AWS Lambda Cold Starts — Minimizing Latency, Respecting Limits
bytes.body: Cold starts occur when Lambda provisions a new execution environment: downloads your package, starts the runtime, and runs initialization code outside your handler. For Node.js this typically adds 200–500ms; for Java with Spring it can hit 3–10s. Provisioned Concurrency eliminates cold starts by keeping environments warm — configure it on a published version or alias, not $LATEST. Lambda's hard timeout ceiling is 15 minutes; anything longer belongs in Step Functions or ECS. Because each invocation may hit a different environment, never rely on in-memory state across calls. Use `/tmp` (512MB–10GB ephemeral, per-environment) only for single-invocation scratch space, not shared cache. Connection pooling to RDS must use RDS Proxy — raw connections held in the handler scope survive warm invocations but leak under concurrency bursts. Keep deployment packages under 50MB zipped; larger packages directly inflate init duration. Use Lambda Layers to share dependencies without bloating each function's package, and enable SnapStart for Java 21 runtimes to snapshot post-init state and restore it on subsequent cold starts.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Provision concurrency on an alias to eliminate cold starts
aws lambda put-provisioned-concurrency-config \
  --function-name my-api \
  --qualifier prod \
  --provisioned-concurrent-executions 10

# Check init duration vs billed duration in a recent invocation
aws logs filter-log-events \
  --log-group-name /aws/lambda/my-api \
  --filter-pattern '"Init Duration"' \
  --query 'events[*].message' \
  --output text | head -5

bytes.topic: Edge Computing — Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge, latency benefits
bytes.title: AWS Edge Computing — Lambda@Edge vs CloudFront Functions for Latency-Critical Apps
bytes.body:
AWS offers two edge compute primitives via CloudFront: CloudFront Functions and Lambda@Edge. Both execute at CloudFront's 400+ Points of Presence instead of your origin region, cutting round-trip latency for globally distributed users — the same latency win Cloudflare Workers and Vercel Edge provide, but within the AWS ecosystem.

CloudFront Functions are sub-millisecond JS runtimes ideal for lightweight work — URL rewrites, header normalization, simple geo-redirects. They hook into viewer-request and viewer-response events only, with a 2MB code limit and ~$0.10/million invocations.

Lambda@Edge runs full Node.js 18.x or Python 3.12 with up to 128MB memory across all four CloudFront events (viewer-request, origin-request, origin-response, viewer-response). The killer use case: JWT validation at the viewer-request stage means auth runs at the nearest PoP — your us-east-1 origin never receives unauthenticated traffic. For users in APAC or Europe, this eliminates a 200–400ms transatlantic round-trip on every authenticated request.

Deploy Lambda@Edge functions exclusively to us-east-1; CloudFront auto-replicates them globally. After updates, run aws cloudfront create-invalidation to force propagation across all PoPs.

bytes.topic: Managed Services — when managed beats self-hosted (Kafka, Redis, Postgres)
bytes.title: AWS Managed Services — When MSK, ElastiCache, and Aurora Beat Self-Hosted
bytes.body:
AWS offers three managed services that consistently outperform self-hosted equivalents at scale: Amazon MSK (Kafka), ElastiCache for Redis, and RDS/Aurora (Postgres).

MSK vs self-hosted Kafka: Running Kafka on EC2 means managing ZooKeeper/KRaft clusters, partition rebalancing, and broker upgrades yourself. MSK handles broker provisioning, automatic storage scaling, and integrates with IAM for SASL/IAM auth — no custom auth servers needed. MSK Serverless eliminates capacity planning entirely.

ElastiCache vs self-managed Redis: ElastiCache Multi-AZ with automatic failover promotes a replica in under 60 seconds. Cluster mode enables horizontal sharding without maintaining Sentinel. Encryption at rest (KMS) and in-transit (TLS) are checkbox configurations, not shell scripts.

RDS/Aurora vs EC2 Postgres: Aurora's storage auto-scales to 128 TiB, replicas share the same storage layer (no replication lag on reads), and Multi-AZ failover completes in ~30 seconds. Cross-region read replicas provision in minutes.

The break-even hits when operational overhead — patching, backup scripts, CloudWatch agent tuning — exceeds the managed service premium. That threshold is typically 3–5 nodes per cluster.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Fetch all three managed service endpoints via AWS CLI
MSK_BOOTSTRAP=$(aws kafka get-bootstrap-brokers \
  --cluster-arn "$MSK_CLUSTER_ARN" \
  --query 'BootstrapBrokerStringSaslIam' --output text)

REDIS_ENDPOINT=$(aws elasticache describe-replication-groups \
  --replication-group-id my-redis \
  --query 'ReplicationGroups[0].NodeGroups[0].PrimaryEndpoint.Address' --output text)

AURORA_ENDPOINT=$(aws rds describe-db-clusters \
  --db-cluster-identifier my-aurora \
  --query 'DBClusters[0].Endpoint' --output text)

echo "MSK (IAM auth): $MSK_BOOTSTRAP"
echo "Redis: ${REDIS_ENDPOINT}:6379"
echo "Aurora writer: ${AURORA_ENDPOINT}:5432"

bytes.topic: Security — encryption at rest/transit, secrets manager, WAF, DDoS protection
bytes.title: AWS Security Hardening — Encryption, Secrets Manager, WAF, and Shield in Practice
bytes.body: AWS encrypts S3, RDS, and EBS at rest via KMS — but you must opt in. Use `aws:kms` as your S3 default encryption algorithm and enforce it with a bucket policy denying `s3:PutObject` unless `x-amz-server-side-encryption: aws:kms` is present. For secrets, never use SSM Parameter Store plain strings — store DB credentials and API keys in Secrets Manager with automatic rotation via the built-in Lambda rotator. Reference them in ECS task definitions using `secrets:` blocks; the value never touches your environment variables as plaintext. In-transit: enforce TLS 1.2+ on ALBs by setting a security policy of `ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06`. For WAF, attach an `AWS::WAFv2::WebACL` to your CloudFront distribution with the `AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet` and `AWSManagedRulesKnownBadInputsRuleSet` — these catch SQLi, XSS, and Log4Shell patterns without custom rules. DDoS protection upgrades from Shield Standard (free, automatic) to Shield Advanced for SLA-backed response and cost protection on Route 53, CloudFront, and ALBs. Enable it per resource, not globally.

bytes.topic: PaaS vs IaaS — Railway/Vercel/Fly.io vs raw AWS/GCP/Azure, DX vs control
bytes.title: AWS IaaS vs PaaS (Railway/Vercel/Fly.io) — What You're Actually Trading
bytes.body:
When you deploy to Fly.io or Railway, you're getting an opinionated wrapper around primitives that AWS exposes raw. Fly.io runs Firecracker VMs — functionally similar to EC2 Nitro — but abstracts the ALB, target groups, security groups, and VPC subnetting behind a single `fly deploy`. Vercel's Edge Network mirrors CloudFront + Lambda@Edge, minus the distribution config, cache behavior rules, and IAM trust policies you'd wire manually.

On raw AWS, a production ECS Fargate service touches: a VPC with public/private subnets, an ALB with listener rules, a target group with health check tuning, an ECS task definition (CPU/mem, log driver, secrets from SSM/Secrets Manager), a service with deployment circuit breakers, and IAM task roles scoped per service. That's real control — blue/green via CodeDeploy, Spot capacity with `capacityProviderStrategy`, cross-account ECR pulls — none of which PaaS exposes.

The break-even point: PaaS wins below ~$500/mo and under 3 engineers. Above that, raw AWS pays back through Reserved Instance pricing, VPC peering to RDS, and Savings Plans. You can't negotiate unit economics with Railway.
bytes.code_snippet:
# PaaS: one command, done
fly deploy --dockerfile Dockerfile

# Raw AWS: equivalent surface area
aws ecs register-task-definition --cli-input-json file://task-def.json
aws ecs create-service \
  --cluster prod \
  --task-definition my-app:7 \
  --desired-count 3 \
  --capacity-provider-strategy '[{"capacityProvider":"FARGATE_SPOT","weight":2},{"capacityProvider":"FARGATE","weight":1}]' \
  --load-balancers '[{"targetGroupArn":"arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:...","containerName":"app","containerPort":8080}]' \
  --deployment-configuration 'deploymentCircuitBreaker={enable=true,rollback=true}'

bytes.topic: Region Selection — latency, data residency, compliance requirements
bytes.title: AWS Region Selection — Latency, Data Residency, and Compliance Done Right
bytes.body: Picking an AWS region isn't just about proximity — it's a three-way tradeoff between latency, data residency laws, and service availability. Start with `aws ec2 describe-regions` to list enabled regions, then use CloudWatch Internet Monitor or Route 53 Latency Routing policies to measure real-world latency from your user base — don't rely on ping alone. For data residency, GDPR mandates EU data stay in EU regions (eu-west-1, eu-central-1, eu-north-1); HIPAA workloads need a BAA with AWS, but any region qualifies if controls are in place. FedRAMP High is limited to us-east-1 and us-west-2 GovCloud variants. Use AWS Config rule `restricted-ec2-region` (or an SCP in AWS Organizations) to enforce region boundaries at the account level — this prevents accidental resource creation outside approved regions. Multi-region architectures should use Global Tables (DynamoDB), Aurora Global Database, or S3 Cross-Region Replication with SSE-KMS. Always verify service availability per region via the AWS Regional Services List before committing to an architecture — not all services are GA everywhere.

bytes.topic: Compliance — SOC2, GDPR, HIPAA considerations per provider
bytes.title: AWS Compliance — SOC2, GDPR, and HIPAA Without the Guesswork
bytes.body:
AWS doesn't make you compliant — it gives you the building blocks. Each framework maps to specific services.

**SOC2:** AWS Artifact provides on-demand SOC2 Type II reports (under Agreements tab). Enable AWS Config with managed rules like `cloudtrail-enabled`, `s3-bucket-logging-enabled`, and `root-account-mfa-enabled`. AWS Security Hub's AWS Foundational Security Best Practices standard automates ~180 controls continuously.

**GDPR:** Use AWS Macie to scan S3 for PII (SSNs, emails, passport numbers) with automated findings routed to EventBridge. Lock data residency via SCP: `aws:RequestedRegion` condition key blocks cross-region writes at the org level. Enable CloudTrail Data Events on S3 to satisfy the right-of-access audit trail.

**HIPAA:** AWS signs a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) through Artifact — required before storing PHI. Only 100+ HIPAA-eligible services qualify (EC2, RDS, S3, Lambda — but not all). Enable encryption in transit (enforce-https bucket policy) and at rest (KMS CMKs with CloudTrail key usage logging). AWS Config's `hipaa-security-indicator` conformance pack deploys 27 rules in one shot.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Deploy AWS Config HIPAA conformance pack
aws configservice put-conformance-pack \
  --conformance-pack-name hipaa-security-pack \
  --template-s3-uri s3://aws-config-rules-us-east-1/conformance-packs/Operational-Best-Practices-for-HIPAA-Security.yaml

# Query non-compliant resources across all HIPAA rules
aws configservice describe-compliance-by-config-rule \
  --compliance-types NON_COMPLIANT \
  --query 'ComplianceByConfigRules[*].[ConfigRuleName,Compliance.ComplianceType]' \
  --output table

# Verify BAA is signed in AWS Artifact
aws artifact get-account-status \
  --query 'AccountStatus.HipaaStatus'

bytes.topic: Deployment Models — blue/green, canary, rolling updates on cloud infra
bytes.title: AWS Deployment Models — Blue/Green, Canary, and Rolling with CodeDeploy & ECS
bytes.body:
AWS offers three core deployment strategies, each with distinct trade-offs on rollback speed and blast radius.

**Blue/Green** spins up a new ECS task set or Auto Scaling Group alongside the existing one. Traffic shifts via an ALB weighted target group rule — 0% to 100% in one cut. CodeDeploy manages this natively with `BlueGreenDeploymentConfiguration`. Rollback is instant: flip the listener rule back.

**Canary** is a subset of blue/green. CodeDeploy's `LINEAR_10PERCENT_EVERY_1MINUTES` or `CANARY_10PERCENT_5MINUTES` deployment configs route a small traffic slice first, monitor CloudWatch alarms, then auto-complete or auto-rollback. Pair with `DeploymentReadyOption: CONTINUE_DEPLOYMENT` and a `terminationWaitTimeInMinutes` buffer.

**Rolling** updates replace tasks in batches via `minimumHealthyPercent` and `maximumPercent` in ECS service config — no duplicate fleet cost. No instant rollback; you re-deploy the previous task definition revision.

For Lambda, use `AWS::Lambda::Alias` with `RoutingConfig.AdditionalVersionWeights` for canary at the function level — no ALB required.
bytes.code_snippet:
# CodeDeploy appspec.yml — ECS canary deployment
version: 0.0
Resources:
  - TargetService:
      Type: AWS::ECS::Service
      Properties:
        TaskDefinition: <TASK_DEF_ARN>
        LoadBalancerInfo:
          ContainerName: app
          ContainerPort: 8080
        PlatformVersion: LATEST
Hooks:
  - BeforeAllowTraffic: ValidateHealthCheckLambda
  - AfterAllowTraffic: SmokeTestLambda

bytes.topic: Multi-cloud & Vendor Lock-in — portability trade-offs, abstraction layers
bytes.title: AWS Multi-Cloud Portability — What Abstraction Layers Actually Cost You
bytes.body:
Running workloads across AWS and GCP/Azure sounds like insurance, but every abstraction layer you add to enable portability degrades your ability to use AWS-native capabilities that justify the bill.

Take compute: if you abstract EC2 behind Terraform's `aws_instance` with generic userdata scripts, you lose Nitro-based features like ENA Express, Graviton3 cost savings, and Placement Groups. Similarly, abstracting S3 behind a generic object-store interface (using boto3's base S3 calls only) means forfeiting S3 Express One Zone, Intelligent-Tiering lifecycle automation, and S3 Object Lambda — features that can cut costs 40–60%.

The real lock-in isn't the SDK — it's your event architecture. EventBridge rules, SNS fan-out to SQS, and Step Functions state machines are deeply AWS-specific. Wrapping these behind a "cloud-agnostic message bus" interface is theoretically portable but operationally brittle.

Practical rule: use Terraform for infrastructure shape (VPCs, IAM, subnets) but call AWS APIs directly in application code. Portability at the infra layer, performance at the app layer.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Terraform: portable infra shape
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "data" {
  bucket = "my-app-data"
}

# App layer: AWS-native, not abstracted
import boto3

s3 = boto3.client("s3")

# Use S3-specific feature — Object Lambda
response = s3.get_object(
    Bucket="my-app-data--ol-s3alias",
    Key="user/profile.json"
)

# Never wrap this in a generic get_object(provider, bucket, key) abstraction
# You'd lose Object Lambda, checksum validation, and request metrics

bytes.topic: Monitoring & Billing — cost anomaly detection, usage dashboards
bytes.title: AWS Cost Anomaly Detection — Catch Billing Surprises Before They Hurt
bytes.body:
AWS Cost Anomaly Detection uses ML models trained on your historical spend to flag unexpected cost spikes — no threshold configuration required. It operates per service, linked account, cost category, or custom cost allocation tag. Once enabled, it evaluates spend continuously and sends alerts via SNS or AWS Budgets Actions.

Pair it with Cost Explorer's hourly granularity (enabled under Preferences) to drill into the exact service and usage type driving the anomaly. The `GetAnomalies` API lets you programmatically pull detected anomalies, filter by date range, and correlate with CloudTrail events to identify the root cause — a rogue Lambda invocation, forgotten NAT Gateway, or EC2 on-demand burst.

For dashboards, CloudWatch's `AWS/Billing` namespace exposes `EstimatedCharges` per service. Use a CloudWatch Dashboard with metric math to compute daily burn rate: `RATE(EstimatedCharges) * 86400`. Set Composite Alarms across multiple services so a single SNS notification fires when combined spend exceeds your SLA. Tag-based cost allocation via Resource Groups gives per-team or per-environment breakdown without a third-party tool.
